items.title: Broadened the draft’s framing of Enterprise IdP to apply to more deployment scenarios
items.body:
Broaden the draft’s framing from “enterprise IdP” to the more precise trust model the protocol actually depends on: the IdP Authorization Server the downstream Resource Authorization Server already trusts for SSO and subject resolution.

This update keeps the protocol semantics intact while making the document align better with the CIAM and agentic examples already in the draft. The revised text consistently clarifies that the Resource Authorization Server still applies its own local policy and issues its own access tokens; ID-JAG changes the grant path, not resource ownership or token issuance authority.

It also tightens the cross-domain guidance for multi-hop deployments. The draft now says an ID-JAG is specific to the issuer-to-RAS trust relationship identified by `aud`, and the same ID-JAG must not be reused as the authorization grant for a different downstream Resource Authorization Server. Later hops may use newly issued ID-JAGs or other mechanisms.

Key edits:
- Update abstract, introduction, roles, and overview to describe the trusted-IdP model instead of enterprise-only framing.
- Preserve existing consent/policy semantics by clarifying that the RAS does not need to obtain user consent directly again at the token exchange step.
- Generalize use-case framing so enterprise remains a primary example, but not the only valid deployment model.
- Refresh CIAM and AI agent text to match the same trust-boundary language.

No protocol flow changes were introduced beyond clarifying the scope of ID-JAG reuse in downstream multi-hop scenarios.

items.title: Add terms section and updated use of JIT to use term
Add support for "authorization_grant_profiles_supported"
items.body:
Register new AS metadata parameter `authorization_grant_profiles_supported` that enables a Resource Authorization Server to publish support for `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-profile:id-jag` profile.  This enables a client to discover at runtime whether a Resource AS supports ID-JAG processing or not as inspecting `grant_type` is not sufficient for assertion grants like `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer`.  

This resolves #16

items.title: Change refresh token langugae that implies id-jags should be long lives
items.body: Id-jag tokens should be encouraged to be short lived, single use artifacts. This language conflicts with that assumption and implies that id-jags may be longer lived than access tokens. We could optionally remove this paragrah

items.title: DPoP Optimisation
items.body:
Initial draft at this proposal, things to discuss include

- Whether we should consider instead of a _supported metadata flag defining another method for client authentication because otherwise an AS cannot force the optimisation in the event it would like to. _Update see latest comment on PR_
- Unrelated but I noticed nbf is defined for the Client Attestation PoP when I dont think its required, suggest we remove this. _Update created #181 to address_
- How should we handle challenge vs nonce in the DPoP proof, technically we could support both.

Fixes #69

items.title: Fix minor editorial nitpicks
items.body:
Few more issues I've noticed
- The example in "3.2.2. Token Endpoint Request" includes `redirect_uri` which is removed
- "A.1. "client_id" Syntax" and "A.2. "client_secret" Syntax" allow client_id and client_secret to be an empty string (`*VSCHAR`)
- Sections "7.1.4. Access Token Privilege Restriction" and "7.3.2. Access Token Privilege Restriction" are near-duplicates.

items.title: Added doc history
Clarify loopback exception for SSRF checks
items.body:
This clarifies that SSRF checks for both the CIMD itself and URLs within it should be handled the same way: don't fetch special use IPs unless everything is running on loopback. 

This change does normatively strengthen the requirements for URLs within the CIMD. My assumption is that the current wording of MUST NOT for the CIMD itself with an exception for running on loopback and SHOULD NOT for contained URLs is meant to accomplish the same thing, and I thought combining the two made it read more clearly.

See also #73.

items.title: Add `software_version` to identify metadata changes
items.body:
The -01 draft suggests that clients should refrain from updating metadata too frequently, and that authorization servers should expect occasional changes to metadata. It might be helpful for clients to make it easy for servers to identify when metadata has changed.

RFC 7591 provides a `software_version` field that we could use for this purpose. This PR introduces a couple of recommendations along these lines:
- Clients may include this field to indicate when the metadata document has changed and/or to differentiate between application versions with different capabilities
- If clients parameterize this in the URL, authorization servers could treat different versions as different clients with different policies.

I briefly chatted about this with Aaron before making this PR, and I am very much open to discussing further!

items.title: Update 'response_type' requirements in documentation
items.body: Clarify the requirements for 'response_type' in OAuth specification. Addresses issue #113.

items.title: Update definition of `req_wl` claim
items.body: Clarified the definition of the `req_wl` claim to specify that it should contain a single workload identifier, with the option for multiple identifiers separated by commas. Addresses issue #311.

items.title: Clarify protection of auth_session value
items.body: Removed the example of using a random string or JWE for protecting the auth_session value. Addresses issue #107

items.title: Enhance security description for authorization challenge
items.body:
Clarified the risks associated with the authorization challenge endpoint by specifying that it can receive low-entropy values like OTPs, and emphasized the need for rate-limiting on such inputs.

For #138

items.title: Update auth_session binding requirements
items.body: Changed 'MUST' to 'SHOULD' for auth_session device binding and added a reference to DPoP binding. Closes #129

items.title: Clarify authorization code definition and update history
items.body: Clarified the definition of authorization code and updated document history. Addresses issue #102

items.title: Revise error codes and author information
items.body: Updated error codes section and modified author details. Addresses issue #103.

items.title: Clarify Authorization Server's redirect-to-web capability
items.body: Added a note about redirecting to a web browser for authorization. Addresses issue #104.

items.title: Refine specifications for Authorization Challenge Request
items.body: Clarify the requirements for the Authorization Challenge Request and Response, and provide a more detailed example for implementation. Addresses issue #108.

items.title: Update limitations section for clarity and intent
items.body: Clarify the scope of the specification and emphasize the intent to profile specific interaction patterns for interoperability. Addresses issue #106.

items.title: Clarify that no changes are required for the Step-up auth spec
items.body: See issue #68

items.title: Enhance security for auth_session binding
items.body: Emphasize the importance of binding the auth_session to the requesting device to prevent session hijacking. Introduce DPoP binding for auth_session protection. Addresses issue #120.

items.title: Specify uniqueness and protection for auth_session
items.body: Add requirement for unique and collision-resistant auth_session values. Addresses issue #119.
